definition: The most probable outcome of fiscal dominance for advanced economies is prolonged moderate inflation (3-6%) and financial repression producing persistently negative real bond returns — NOT crisis or hyperinflation. This 'boring middle scenario' generates the most significant wealth transfer from creditors to debtors over 10-20 years.
evidence: Systematic tabulation: 4/7 episodes produced moderate inflation (3-6%) with negative real returns
0/5 advanced economy, own-currency-issuer episodes resulted in default or hyperinflation
Pair_0 verdict: generalist_02 'decisively stronger' on this point
Post-WWII UK: 270% to 50% over 40 years via repression + growth
Japan: 250%+ for 30 years, stagnation not crisis
Italy: 120-155% for 30+ years with ECB backstop
17% acute crisis rate, all in non-monetary-sovereign contexts
4/7 historical fiscal dominance episodes produced financial repression
0/5 for default or hyperinflation in own-currency issuers
UK gilt crisis (2022) demonstrated markets can enforce real-rate discipline even on own-currency issuers
Historical sample conflates capital controls/regulated banking with modern capital mobility
open_questions: Can the US sustain financial repression across 6-8 presidential election cycles under deep polarization?
Does the 'stealth' requirement for the boring middle scenario conflict with modern information transparency?
What is the cumulative wealth transfer magnitude under 10-20 years of 3-6% inflation?
Can financial repression operate effectively in modern liberalized capital markets?
Do crypto, stablecoins, and digital capital mobility structurally weaken the repression toolkit?
Is the post-WWII deleveraging toolkit still available given reversed demographics and absence of productivity boom?
Do modern capital mobility conditions invalidate historical analogues?
What form would financial repression take in current institutional context?
Is the 50% probability estimate defensible or does modern capital mobility reduce it?
